class_schedule.topic: Fundamentals of Healthcare IT Project Management
learning_objectives: Apply project management fundamentals to healthcare IT contexts
Understand why healthcare IT projects have unique risks and constraints
Compare waterfall, agile, and hybrid approaches for health IT
readings: Project Management in Healthcare Informatics Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Healthcare IT projects fail at alarming rates. Poor project management is the leading cause. This course equips you with the skills to plan, execute, and deliver complex health IT initiatives on time and on budget.
key_concepts: Healthcare IT project types: EHR implementation, system upgrade, integration, analytics platform
Unique healthcare constraints: patient safety, 24/7 operations, regulatory requirements, clinical stakeholders
Project lifecycle: initiation, planning, execution, monitoring/control, closure
Methodology selection: waterfall for regulated projects, agile for iterative development, hybrid for most health IT
Project management knowledge areas applied to healthcare
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study comparison: a successful Epic implementation vs. a failed EHR project, identifying project management factors
setup_requirements: Project management software (MS Project, Smartsheet, or Jira)

review.value: Studies estimate 30-70% of health IT projects fail to meet their objectives, often due to poor stakeholder management, scope creep, and inadequate change management
Combining waterfall's structure (for regulatory and infrastructure work) with agile's flexibility (for clinical workflow configuration and user-facing features)

learning_objectives: Develop a project charter for a healthcare IT initiative
Identify and analyze stakeholders in healthcare IT projects
Create stakeholder engagement strategies for clinical and technical audiences
readings: Project Management in Healthcare Informatics Ch. 3

lecture_notes.introduction: Project success begins with a clear charter and engaged stakeholders. In healthcare, stakeholder management is particularly complex because clinicians, administrators, IT staff, and patients all have different needs and power dynamics.
key_concepts: Project charter: business case, objectives, scope, constraints, high-level timeline, budget
Stakeholder identification: sponsor, steering committee, clinical champions, end users, patients
Stakeholder analysis: power/interest grid, influence mapping
Clinical champion development: identifying and empowering clinician advocates
Communication planning: tailoring messages for clinical vs. technical vs. executive audiences
lecture_notes.demonstration: Develop a project charter and stakeholder analysis for a patient portal enhancement project

review.value: A respected clinician who advocates for the project, helps design workflows, provides peer credibility, and supports adoption among clinical staff
Tool plotting stakeholders by their power to influence the project and their interest in it, guiding engagement strategy (manage closely, keep satisfied, keep informed, monitor)

class_schedule.topic: Project Planning: Scope, Schedule, and Budget
learning_objectives: Create a work breakdown structure for a health IT project
Develop a project schedule with dependencies and milestones
Estimate and manage budgets for healthcare IT projects
readings: Project Management in Healthcare Informatics Ch. 5-6

lecture_notes.introduction: Detailed planning prevents the chaos that sinks health IT projects. A well-built WBS, realistic schedule, and honest budget set the foundation for successful delivery.
key_concepts: Work Breakdown Structure (WBS): decomposing project into manageable work packages
Schedule development: task sequencing, dependency types (FS, SS, FF, SF), critical path
Estimation techniques: analogous, parametric, three-point (PERT), bottom-up
Healthcare IT budget components: software licensing, hardware, consulting, training, travel, contingency
Go-live scheduling: avoiding holiday periods, considering clinical volume patterns
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a WBS and Gantt chart for an EHR module implementation project with realistic healthcare timelines

review.value: The longest sequence of dependent tasks determining the minimum project duration - any delay on the critical path delays the entire project
Healthcare go-lives avoid flu season, holiday periods, and academic year starts; often target spring or fall when staffing is stable

learning_objectives: Identify and assess risks specific to healthcare IT projects
Develop risk mitigation strategies and contingency plans
Manage scope creep and change requests in clinical environments
readings: Project Management in Healthcare Informatics Ch. 8

lecture_notes.introduction: Healthcare IT projects carry unique risks including patient safety, regulatory compliance, clinical workflow disruption, and provider resistance. Proactive risk management is essential.
key_concepts: Healthcare IT risk categories: technical, clinical, organizational, regulatory, vendor
Risk assessment: probability x impact matrix, qualitative and quantitative analysis
Risk response strategies: avoid, mitigate, transfer, accept
Scope management: change control board, impact assessment, scope creep prevention
Contingency planning: rollback procedures, go-live delay criteria, parallel operations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a risk assessment workshop for an EHR go-live, identifying top 10 risks with mitigation plans

review.value: Pre-defined conditions that must be met before proceeding with go-live: training completion rates, testing pass rates, downtime procedure readiness
Committee that reviews, approves, or rejects change requests to prevent scope creep while allowing necessary adjustments

class_schedule.topic: Change Management and Training for Health IT
learning_objectives: Apply change management frameworks to health IT implementations
Design training programs for clinical end users
readings: Project Management in Healthcare Informatics Ch. 10
Kotter's 8-Step Change Model Applied to Healthcare

lecture_notes.introduction: Technology change in healthcare fails more often from people problems than technical problems. Change management and training are the difference between a system that gets used and one that gets worked around.
key_concepts: Kotter's 8 steps applied to health IT: urgency, coalition, vision, communication, empowerment, wins, consolidation, anchoring
ADKAR model: Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability, Reinforcement
Training modalities: classroom, e-learning, simulation lab, at-the-elbow, super user support
Training by role: physicians, nurses, pharmacists, coders, administrators
Adoption metrics: system usage rates, workaround identification, satisfaction surveys
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design a training program for a CPOE implementation: curricula by role, training environment setup, competency assessment, and go-live support plan

review.value: Individual change model: Awareness (why change), Desire (want to change), Knowledge (how to change), Ability (demonstrate new skills), Reinforcement (sustain the change)
Go-live support strategy placing trained support staff alongside clinicians during initial system use to provide immediate, in-context assistance

learning_objectives: Apply earned value management to track healthcare IT project performance
Conduct effective project status reporting for executive stakeholders
Execute project closure activities including lessons learned
readings: Project Management in Healthcare Informatics Ch. 11-12

lecture_notes.introduction: Execution is where plans meet reality. Monitoring performance, communicating status honestly, and closing projects formally are disciplines that distinguish successful project managers.
key_concepts: Earned Value Management: PV, EV, AC, SPI, CPI for objective performance tracking
Status reporting: RAG dashboards, burn-down charts, executive summaries
Issue and decision logs: tracking and resolving blockers
Go-live command center: real-time issue management during cutover
Project closure: lessons learned, benefits realization, transition to operations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculate EVM metrics for a health IT project at week 8, interpret the results, and present a status report to the steering committee

review.value: SPI = EV/PV: SPI > 1 means ahead of schedule, SPI < 1 means behind. A critical metric for health IT projects with fixed go-live dates
Structured retrospective capturing what went well, what did not, and recommendations for future projects - critical for organizational learning

questions.question: Explain why clinical champion identification and development is critical for healthcare IT project success. Describe the ideal characteristics of a clinical champion and how a project manager should engage them.
questions.answer: Clinical champions are critical because clinicians trust peers more than IT staff or administrators. A respected physician or nurse advocating for the system is far more persuasive than any project communication. Ideal characteristics: (1) respected by peers for clinical competence, (2) early adopter of technology, (3) willing to invest time in training and advocacy, (4) credible and influential in their department, (5) able to bridge clinical and technical language. Engagement approach: involve champions early in design decisions so they have ownership, provide advanced training so they become experts, give them visible roles during go-live, recognize their contribution publicly, and protect their clinical time for project activities.

rubric.key: Clear rationale for clinical champion importance

questions.question: A hospital plans to go live with a new EHR in October. The project has a critical path of 38 weeks. It is currently January 16. Calculate the latest possible start date and explain three healthcare-specific factors that should influence go-live date selection.
questions.answer: With an October go-live target (assume October 1) and 38-week critical path, counting back 38 weeks from October 1 lands on approximately January 8. Since today is January 16, the project is already 8 days behind the latest start date and at immediate risk. Healthcare-specific factors: (1) Avoid flu season (November-March) - ED and hospital volumes spike, making staff less available for training and more stressed during transition. (2) Avoid academic year transitions (July in teaching hospitals) - new residents starting creates an already chaotic period, compounding EHR transition difficulty. (3) Consider clinical volume patterns - mid-spring or early fall typically have moderate, predictable volumes. Also consider: major holiday proximity, fiscal year alignment for budget, and staff vacation patterns.

rubric.key: Identifies schedule risk based on current date

questions.question: Apply the ADKAR change management model to an EHR implementation. For each element, describe a specific activity the project team should undertake to move clinicians through that stage of change.
questions.answer: ADKAR applied to EHR implementation: (1) Awareness: conduct townhall meetings explaining why the new EHR is needed - current system risks, regulatory requirements, improvement opportunities. Share data on current system limitations. (2) Desire: identify clinical champions who advocate for the change, address WIIFM ('what's in it for me') for each role - show how the new EHR will reduce documentation time or improve patient safety. (3) Knowledge: deliver role-based training curricula in the EHR training environment, covering workflows specific to each user role. (4) Ability: provide simulation lab practice with realistic patient scenarios, followed by at-the-elbow support during the first weeks of go-live so clinicians can apply knowledge in real situations. (5) Reinforcement: track adoption metrics, celebrate early wins publicly, address ongoing issues promptly, and provide optimization training after initial stabilization.

rubric.key: Specific healthcare IT activities for each element

questions.question: During an EHR implementation, a department chair demands that a new clinical dashboard be added to the project scope three weeks before go-live. As project manager, how do you handle this?
questions.answer: Response approach: (1) Acknowledge the request respectfully - the dashboard may have clinical value. (2) Follow the change control process: document the request formally as a change request with business justification, scope description, and estimated impact. (3) Conduct impact assessment: evaluate the effect on timeline (critical path analysis), budget (development hours), resources (developer availability), testing requirements, and training implications. (4) Present the assessment to the Change Control Board with options: (a) add to scope and delay go-live, (b) add to scope with reduced functionality, (c) defer to Phase 2 post-go-live optimization, (d) decline. (5) Recommend option (c): defer to Phase 2, because adding scope three weeks before go-live introduces significant risk - untested functionality, insufficient training time, and potential go-live delay affecting hundreds of users. (6) Document the decision and communicate to the department chair with a committed Phase 2 timeline.

major_assignments.title: Healthcare IT Project Plan and Execution Simulation
major_assignments.description: Develop a comprehensive project plan for a healthcare IT initiative and execute a simulated project lifecycle including initiation, planning, risk management, change management, and lessons learned.
objectives: Create a professional project plan for a healthcare IT initiative
Demonstrate project management skills through simulation exercises
Apply change management and stakeholder engagement techniques
Conduct project closure with lessons learned
deliverables: Detailed project plan: WBS, schedule, budget, risk register
Simulated status reports and EVM analysis

criteria: Realistic schedule with dependencies and critical path
Effective change management and training strategy

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of project management concepts and healthcare IT application

class_participation.description: Active participation in project simulation exercises and case discussions

major_assignments.description: Healthcare IT project plan and execution simulation

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment of project initiation, planning, and risk management

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including change management, execution, and project closure

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance expected. Simulation exercises require active participation. More than 3 unexcused absences may reduce grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late project deliverables lose 10% per day. Project milestones must be met for iterative feedback.
course_policies.collaboration: Simulation exercises are team-based. Project plans must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All project plans must be original. Project management templates may be used but content must be the student's own.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Project scheduling software (MS Project, Smartsheet, or free alternative), spreadsheet software, presentation software.
